text: Our patient is a twenty-nine-year-old female with a one-year history of laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass with a hundred pounds of excessive weight loss. Her BMI is thirty-three. She presents to the emergency department with complaints of severe epigastric abdominal pain, ten out of ten, nausea and vomiting for more than twelve hours.

Preoperative studies included a CT scan showing mesenteric edema, swirling of the central mesentery and a small amount of high density pelvic free fluid. The patient was scheduled for a diagnostic laparoscopy.

The patient is positioned supine with legs open and arms tucked. Three trocars are inserted: a 12mm trocar in the right upper quadrant, and two 5mm trocars in the right flank and right lower quadrant.

The surgeon explores the abdominal cavity, confirming an antacolic gastric bypass. Dilation of mesenteric lymphatic channels and an increased amount of lymphatic fluid are visible. The bowel appears twisted and incarcerated within the mesenteric defect at the JJ anastomosis site.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to take down an adhesion, fully reducing the small bowel from its incarcerated position.

The surgeon runs the alimentary limb proximally to identify the Peterson space. The greater omentum is mobilized, followed by the transverse colon being mobilized from each side of the Peterson space.

The surgeon runs the bowel distally to identify the ligament of Treitz. The biliary limb is then traced until reaching the JJ anastomosis. The mesenteric opening at the JJ anastomosis is closed using a 3-0 silk suture in a running fashion.
The surgeon runs the alimentary limb until reaching Peterson's defect, which is then closed using a running three-oh silk suture.
The surgeon irrigates and aspirates the abdominal cavity, removing excess fluid and ensuring a clear surgical field.
Estimated blood loss less than five cc's. The patient was discharged thirty-six hours after surgery with no complications. The three month follow up was unremarkable.
